coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated ultrathin nickel hydroxide nanosheets for high-performance hybrid supercapacitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ji, Zhenyuan
Li, Na
Zhang, Yifei
Xie, Minghua
Shen, Xiaoping
Chen, Lizhi
Xu, Keqiang
Zhu, Guoxing
coredata.dc:description: Owing to their unique electronic properties and capability to host diverse functional groups on the surface, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in energy storage field. Herein, ultrathin nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) decorated nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) nanosheets were fabricated by a facile hydrothermal approach, where NCDs play an important role as a structure-directing agent in tuning the size and thickness of the Ni(OH)2. With the merits of the battery-type faradaic behavior of Ni(OH)2 and high electronic properties of NCDs, the achieved Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets show a specific capacitance of about 1711.2 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, which istwice morethan that of pristine Ni(OH)2. A hybrid supercapacitor device was constructed using Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets as the positive electrode and three-dimensional graphene (3DGE) as the negative electrode, respectively, which presents high energy density of 23.8–34.6 Wh kg−1 at power density of 700–7000 W kg−1. The enhanced electrochemical properties of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanocomposite hold potential for future high-performance energy storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nickel hydroxide
Two-dimensional nanostructures
Synthesis
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples; (b) Raman spectrum of Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanosheets, and the inset is the digital photographs of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 samples.
XPS spectra of Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanosheets: (a) survey scan of the sample, (b) Ni 2p, (c) C 1s and (d) N 1s.
(a) FESEM and (b) TEM images of Ni(OH)2 nanosheets; (c) FESEM, (d, e) TEM and (f) HRTEM images of Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanosheets.
Elemental mapping images of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanosheets.

(a) CV curves of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets at the scan rate of 25 mV s−1; (b) Charge-discharge profile of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets at the current density of 1 A g−1; (c) Charge-discharge profile of Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanosheets at various current densities; (d) The specific capacitance values of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets calculated by the discharge curves.

CV curves of Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE hybrid supercapacitor device at different scan rates; (b) Charge-discharge profile of the device at different current densities; (c) The specific capacitances of the device calculated by the discharge curves; (d) Cycling stability of the device at a current density of 5 A g−1.
(a) Ragone plot of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE hybrid supercapacitor device; (b) A photograph of LED indicator lightened by the assembled device.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots decorated ultrathin nickel hydroxide nanosheets for high-performance hybrid supercapacitor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to their unique electronic properties and capability to host diverse functional groups on the surface, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted increasing attention in energy storage field. Herein, ultrathin nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) decorated nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) nanosheets were fabricated by a facile hydrothermal approach, where NCDs play an important role as a structure-directing agent in tuning the size and thickness of the Ni(OH)2. With the merits of the battery-type faradaic behavior of Ni(OH)2 and high electronic properties of NCDs, the achieved Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets show a specific capacitance of about 1711.2 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1, which istwice morethan that of pristine Ni(OH)2. A hybrid supercapacitor device was constructed using Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets as the positive electrode and three-dimensional graphene (3DGE) as the negative electrode, respectively, which presents high energy density of 23.8–34.6 Wh kg−1 at power density of 700–7000 W kg−1. The enhanced electrochemical properties of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanocomposite hold potential for future high-performance energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:para: To meet the ever-increasing energy demand, it is of crucial importance to develop economical and high-efficient energy storage systems. As a new type of electrochemical energy storage devices, supercapacitor has attracted great interest by virtue of their wide working temperature scope, high power density and long cycle lifespan [1–4]. It is well accepted that the capacitive performance greatly relies on the electrode materials used in supercapacitors [5–7]. In recent years, two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures are regarded as ideal electrode materials in supercapacitors owing to their fascinating electrochemical and mechanical properties [8,9]. Particularly, the atomically thin 2D nanomaterials feature more exposed active sites due to complete exposure of the surface atoms, which provide a promising possibility to tune the capacitance performances of the electrode materials [10,11]. Recently, metal hydroxides especially nickel-based compounds have been extensively studied as battery-type electrodes in hybrid supercapacitors owing to their natural abundance, 2D layered structures, and large theoretical specific capacitances [12–14]. However, the poor intrinsic conductivity of pristine metal hydroxides hinders the desired fast charge transfer, and severely limits their practical application [15–17].

To tackle the above problem, one elegant strategy is to incorporate metal hydroxides into nanocomposites with specific carbon materials, taking advantage of relatively high electron conductivity and electric double-layer capacitance of carbon-based materials. Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging carbon material, have gained considerable interest due to their unique electronic properties and ability to host diverse elements on the surface [18]. Recently, CDs-based nanocomposites have been successfully applied in supercapacitors as electrode materials. In these nanocomposites, CDs can provide more interspaces and pathways for charge transport during charge-discharge processes, which leads to the improvement of rate capability [19]. On the other hand, incorporation of other elements such as nitrogen into CDs can promote the electrochemical reaction with electrolyte, which is effective to achieve improved pseudocapacitance performance [20]. Although some progresses have been made on CDs-based electrode materials for supercapacitor, there is still a large space for improvement of specific capacity of the CDs-based electrode materials.

The main goal of this work is to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) modified ultrathin nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) nanosheets, and further improve the energy storage performance of Ni(OH)2 for practical applications. Herein, NCDs were firstly synthesized, and then employed them to induce the formation of ultrathin Ni(OH)2 nanosheets. Compared with pristine Ni(OH)2, the as-obtained Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanocomposites present smaller size and thinner thickness. Moreover, the addition of a suitable amount of NCDs can optimize the electrochemical specific capacitance of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanocomposites. The hybrid supercapacitor device fabricated by using Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets as positive electrode and three-dimensional graphene (3DGE) as negative electrode shows a high energy density.

Natural flake graphite (45 µm, 99.95%) was obtained from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). All of the other reagents employed in this work are analytical grade, obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and used as received without further purification. The NCDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors, which were heated at 220 °C for 12 h according to the literature [21].

In a typical synthesis of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets, 0.5 g of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and a certain amount of NCDs were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water. The pH value of the solution was then adjusted to 11 with 3 M NaOH solution. After that, the suspension was transferred into a 30 mL of Teflon-lined autoclave and subsequently heated at 180 °C for 24 h. The solid products were separated by centrifugation, thoroughly washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 45 °C for 12 h. The products were designated as Ni(OH)2, Ni(OH)2/NCDs-1, Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs-3 for the feeding amount of 0, 10, 20 and 30 mg of NCDs, respectively.

The crystalline phases of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets were analyzed by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) at a scanning rate of 7° min−1. Raman spectra were performed on a DXR Raman spectrometer with 532 nm laser light. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI spectrometer. The composition of the as-prepared nanosheets was evaluated by elemental analyzer (FLASH1112A) and inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, Vista-MXP, Varian). The morphology and size of the products were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on JSM-7001F microscope and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on JEM-2100 microscope. Elemental mapping analysis was performed on Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin TEM microscope. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the sample was tested using Micromeritics ASAP 2460 sorption analyzer.

Electrochemical measurements were carried out using a CHI 760D Electrochemical Workstation (Chen Hua Instruments, China) in 3.0 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and platinum electrode were used as reference and counter electrode, respectively. The working electrodes were fabricated by mixing electroactive materials, acetylene black and poly (vinylidene fluoride) binder with a weight ratio of 80:10:10 in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone solvent. After stirring for about 24 h, the formed slurry was pasted onto a cleaned nickel foam (surface area 1 cm2). The nickel foam was dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a vacuum oven, and then pressed to a thin foil at 10 MPa. The mass of the material on nickel foam was about 2.0 mg.

To fabricate a hybrid supercapacitor device, Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets and 3DGE were used as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively, with a NKK MPF30AC separator (Nippon Kodoshi Corporation). The 3DGE was synthesized according to the literature [22]. The preparation of the 3DGE electrode was the same as that of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets. The mass ratio was optimized by the following relationship:
(1)
m -
/
m +
=
C +
Δ
V +
/
C -
Δ
V -
where m is the mass of the electroactive materials in the electrode, C and △V represent the specific capacitance value and the potential range of the electrode [23]. The electrodes and the separator were dipped first in a 3 M KOH solution for 10 min and then taken out and assembled double electrode sandwich structure cell. To avoid the evaporation of electrolyte, the whole device was covered by two pieces of plastic sheet and sealed with a closing machine. For clarity, a schematic illustration of the supercapacitor device was supplied in Fig. S1.

The crystal structures of the as-obtained Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets were firstly examined by XRD measurements. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the characteristic peaks at 2θ values of about 19.3°, 33.1°, 38.5°, 52.1°, 59.0°, 62.7°, 69.3°, 70.5°, 72.7° correspond to the (0 0 1), (1 0 0), (1 0 1), (1 0 2), (1 1 0), (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (1 0 3) and (2 0 1) crystal faces of the hexagonal phase of Ni(OH)2 (JCPDS no. 14–0117), indicating that Ni(OH)2 has been successfully synthesized. The Ni(OH)2/NCDs and Ni(OH)2 show similar XRD patterns, and no diffraction peak of NCDs can be observed, which is due to its poor crystallinity and/or low content in the Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets [24]. As shown in Fig. S2, the Raman spectra of NCDs and natural flake graphite show two characteristic peaks at approximately 1380 and 1580 cm−1, which are attributed to the well-defined D and G bands of carbon-based materials, respectively [25]. It is known that the G band is assigned to the E 2g phonon of sp2 carbon atoms, while the D band demonstrates defects and disorders in the hexagonal graphitic layers [26,27]. Comparing with pristine natural graphite, the broader of G band and stronger D band suggest the decrease of the size of the in-plane sp2 domains and a higher level of defect and disorder of the graphitic layers in NCDs. In order to confirm the presence of NCDs, the as-obtained Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets was further analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 1b, the Raman spectrum of Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 also exhibits characteristic D and G bands of NCDs, which indicates the presence of NCDs in the Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets. Furthermore, the photographs (the inset in Fig. 1b) exhibit that the color of Ni(OH)2 changes from blue-green to dark brown after incorporation with NCDs, which also implies that the NCDs have been successfully modified on the surface of Ni(OH)2. In order to determine the exact concentration of each element in the nanocomposites, elemental analysis and inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) were carried out. According to the element analysis, the contents of C, N and H were 3.82 ± 0.23, 2.71 ± 0.09 and 2.33 ± 0.15 wt%, respectively. The content of Ni in Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 nanocomposites was determined to be 57.50 ± 0.35 wt% according to ICP-OES analysis. Thus, the calculated O content was 33.64 ± 0.82 wt%.

The chemical states of Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets were investigated by XPS analysis. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2 a) indicates the as-prepared Ni(OH)2/NCDs sample mainly contain Ni, O, C, and N elements. From the fine-resolution Ni 2p spectrum shown in Fig. 2b, two spin-orbit doublets and two shake-up satellites can be observed. The binding-energy peaks located at 855.7 and 873.4 eV correspond to Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2, which are the characteristics of the Ni(II) state [9]. Fig. 2c displays the de-convoluted XPS spectrum of C 1s, which reveals the surface function groups on the NCDs. The C 1s spectrum can be deconvoluted into three components. The peaks located at the binding energies of 284.7, 286.1 and 288.0 eV can be assigned to CC/CC, oxygenated carbon and nitrous carbon, respectively [21]. As shown in Fig. 2d, core-level XPS analysis of N in Ni(OH)2/NCDs display three different types of nitrides at 399.1, 399.9, and 401.1 eV, which can be attributed to pyridinic nitrogen, pyrrolic nitrogen, and NH, respectively [28,29]. The results of XPS analysis further indicate the coexistence of Ni(OH)2 and NCDs in the Ni(OH)2/NCDs composites.

A three-electrode system was first employed to evaluate the electrochemical performance of the present Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets by means of cycle voltammetry (CV) at a potential range from 0 to 0.6 V. Fig. 5 a presents the CV curves of different electrodes in 3 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 25 mV s−1. Clearly, all of the curves show a pair of oxidation and reduction peaks, which reveal the typical battery-type characteristic of Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets. It is well-accepted that the redox reactions of Ni(OH)2-based materials can be described in the following equations [32–34]:
(2) Ni(OH)2 + OH− → NiOOH + H2O + e−

Moreover, there is a notable difference in the CV loop areas observed among Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes. The area under the CV profile for the Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes is larger than that for bare Ni(OH)2. Since the CV loop area is proportional to specific capacitance of electrode materials, such electrochemical properties indicates that the Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes exhibit enhanced capacity, and the Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 is the most promising material as supercapacitor electrodes among all these samples. To further estimate the electrochemical performance of the as-prepared Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes, the galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements were carried out at the voltage window between 0 and 0.4 V, and the results are shown in Fig. 5b. The plateaus can be observed in Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets, further indicating the battery-type characteristics of these electrodes. Obviously, Ni(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes exhibit different charge/discharge time at the same current density, which suggests the different specific capacitances. The specific capacitance (C s) can be estimated in accordance with the relationship C s = I△t/(m△V), where C s (F g−1) represents the specific capacitance of the electrodes, I (mA) is the charge and discharge current, △t (s) is the discharge time, △V (V) is the potential window during discharge, and m (mg) is the mass of the electroactive materials in the electrode. The calculated specific capacitance values at a current density of 1 A g−1 for Ni(OH)2, Ni(OH)2/NCDs-1, Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 and Ni(OH)2/NCDs-3 electrodes are about 778.6 ± 20.4, 925.7 ± 26.8, 1711.2 ± 48.8 and 1396.3 ± 21.3 F g−1, respectively. Obviously, Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrodes display much higher specific capacitances than bare Ni(OH)2 electrode, and Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 has the largest capacitance among all these electrodes. To understand the rate capability of the electrodes, galvanostatic charge-discharge analysis was carried out at various current densities, as shown in Fig. 5c. The discharge time decreases with the increased current densities ranging from 1 to 10 A g−1. In addition, no visibly shape change can be observed among the profile at different current densities, which suggests that the Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2 electrode possesses good reversibility and rate capability. The specific capacitance values estimated from the galvanostatic charge-discharge profile of the as-prepared electrodes at various current densities are plotted in Fig. 5d. All plots descend with the increasing current density due to the insufficient transfer time for ions between the electrode and the electrolyte [35]. The specific capacitances increase in the following order: Ni(OH)2 < Ni(OH)2/NCDs-1 < Ni(OH)2/NCDs-3 < Ni(OH)2/NCDs-2. This result indicates the importance of NCDs in the nanocomposites. The improved specific capacitance of Ni(OH)2 after decorated with NCDs can be due to the greater electrical conductivity, surface wettability or charge-transfer efficiency of NCDs [19,36]. However, excessive NCDs will result in the reduction of specific capacitance owing to the decreased content of active material (Ni(OH)2) for capacitance generation. Thus, a suitable feeding amount of NCDs is vital to optimizing the specific capacitance of Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrode.

In order to further assess the Ni(OH)2/NCDs electrode for practical applications, a hybrid supercapacitor device was fabricated by using Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets and 3DGE as the positive and negative electrode, respectively. The electrochemical performance (CV and galvonostatic charge/discharge) of the 3DGE are given in Fig. S7. According to the galvonostatic charge/discharge curves of the positive and negative electrode acquired in the three electrode cell, the ideal mass ratio of 3DGE and Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets is estimated to be about 3.5 to balance their charges. Fig. 6 a shows the CV curves of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE hybrid supercapacitor tested at various scan rates. The quasi-rectangular shapes of these CV curves demonstrate the capacitive characteristic of the as-prepared Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE supercapacitor [37]. The CV curves retain the same shape with increasing scan rate, which suggests a outstanding rate capability of the supercapacitor. Fig. 6b exhibits the galvonostatic charge/discharge curves of the hybrid supercapacitor device at different current densities with voltage window from 0 to 1.4 V. The nearly symmetrical shape of the curves verifies the high electrochemical reversibility and coulombic efficiency [9]. The calculated specific capacitances are shown in Fig. 6c. The specific capacitances of Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE supercapacitor at 1, 2, 5 and 10 A g−1 are about 127.0 ± 4.8, 110.0 ± 3.4, 95.9 ± 2.9 and 87.4 ± 3.1 F g−1, respectively. The cycling stability of the Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE device was also evaluated by repeating charge/discharge measurement at 5 A g−1. It is shown that the specific capacitance gradually decreases within the first 1000 cycles and then was almost unchanged. About 74.9% of the specific capacitance was retained even after 5000 continuous cycles. The decrease of the capacitance during the charge/discharge processes could be attributed to the quick decay of electroactive surface area of Ni(OH)2 resulting from the instability of microstructure upon fast and repeated charging/discharging [38,39].

Energy and power densities are important factors for evaluating the electrochemical performance of a hybrid supercapacitor, and the Ragone plot of Ni(OH)2/NCDs//3DGE is shown in Fig. 7 a. The hybrid supercapacitor device delivers a high energy density of about 34.6 ± 1.31 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 700 W kg−1, and can still maintain about 23.8 ± 0.84 Wh kg−1 at 7000 W kg−1. The obtained values are comparable to those of the recently reported devices, such as Ni(OH)2//AC [23], Ni(OH)2//HPC [40], Ni(OH)2@3D Ni//AC [41], CoMoO4/Ni(OH)2//AC [42], Ni(OH)2-CoQD//RGO [43], CQDs/NiCo2O4//AC [44], and GO/CDs/PPy//GO/CDs/PPy [45] (Table S1). In addition, to further assess the practical application of the prepared hybrid supercapacitor device, the hybrid supercapacitor was employed to power a light-emitting diode (LED, 3 mm diameter). As shown in Fig. 7b, the red LED indicator with lowest working voltage of about 2.2 V can be efficiently powered by two hybrid supercapacitor devices in parallel for about 3 min. Therefore, the Ni(OH)2/NCDs is a promising electrode material for energy storage devices. Compared with previous reports, our synthesis route and obtained Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets have several features. (1) We prepared ultrathin Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets through a facile hydrothermal approach, which is not reported previously. (2) The source materials used in the synthesis are common reagents, and water is used as the solvent, which is green and cheap. (3) The synthesis of Ni(OH)2/NCDs with high yield make it possible to get mass production. In addition, the obtained Ni(OH)2/NCDs with enhanced specific capacitance has advantages for energy storage applications.

In summary, a facile hydrothermal method has been proposed to synthesize uniform nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) decorated nickel hydroxide (Ni(OH)2) nanosheets. It is found that NCDs play an important role as a structure-directing agent in tuning the size and thickness of the Ni(OH)2. When used as electrode material for supercapacitor, the as-obtained Ni(OH)2/NCDs nanosheets delivers enhanced specific capacitance of about 1711.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The enhanced capacitor performance is attributed to the properties and ultra-small size of NCDs. More importantly, the hybrid supercapacitor device based on Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets and 3DGE exhibits a high energy density of about 34.6 Wh kg−1 at power density of 700 W kg−1, which is comparable to those of the recently reported devices [23,40–45]. This result demonstrates that the Ni(OH)2/NCDs ultrathin nanosheets are promising electrode materials for high-performance supercapacitors.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51602129 and 21875091), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (Nos. BK20161343 and BK20171295), the Joint Open Fund of Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Ecological Building Material and Environmental Protection Equipments and Key Laboratory for Advanced Technology in Environmental Protection of Jiangsu Province (JH201804), and the Youth Talent Cultivation Plan of Jiangsu University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.02.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
